section: Microstructural Evolution and Mechanical Properties of Nb-Containing Medium-Mn Low-Density Steels

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the abstract and introduction of a study on Nb microalloying in Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C low-density steels. Two variants with 0.02 and 0.04 wt% Nb were investigated in as-cast condition and after aging at 723 K, 773 K, and 823 K. Key findings include: 0.04Nb steel exhibits about half the austenite grain size of 0.02Nb steel due to NbC precipitation; Nb raises the formation energy of κ-carbide, impeding its coarsening, resulting in finer intragranular κ‑carbides and no coarsened intergranular κ‑carbides in either variant aged at 723–823 K. Despite slightly reduced κ‑carbide strengthening, 0.04Nb steel has a yield strength ≈71 MPa higher than 0.02Nb steel, attributed to fine‑grain and dislocation strengthening. Microstructure was characterized by SEM/EBSD and TEM, and thermodynamic/first‑principles calculations were employed. A previous work on cold‑rolled Fe‑12Mn‑9Al‑1.4C‑3Cr (1.5 GPa tensile strength, 15% elongation) is mentioned. Subsequent sections should detail processing, microstructural evolution, mechanical testing, and quantitative strengthening analysis.
properties: 0.04Nb steel yield strength ≈71 MPa higher than 0.02Nb steel
mechanisms: Nb increases κ‑carbide formation energy hindering coarsening

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the remaining introduction, the full experimental section, and the beginning of results (Section 3.1.1) for Nb-containing low-density steels. The study designed Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C alloys with 0.02 and 0.04 wt% Nb, prepared by centrifugal casting under near-rapid solidification (strip dimensions 60×70×2.5 mm) and aged at 723, 773, and 823 K for 3 h. Actual chemical compositions are provided in Table 1. Experimental procedures include XRD (30°–100°), SEM/EBSD, EPMA, TEM, Vickers hardness, and tensile tests (strain rate 2.5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹, gauge 4×12 mm); DFT calculations (VASP, PBE, 500 eV cutoff, U=2.59 eV for Fe) are described. Initial microstructure results: XRD confirms austenite + ferrite phases with no significant peak changes after aging. SEM shows irregular ferrite dispersed in austenite; quantitative image analysis gives ferrite volume fractions ~16.2–18.3 vol% for 0.02Nb and 16.3–18.4 vol% for 0.04Nb, independent of aging temperature. Nb addition results in submicron NbC particles (TEM SAED/EDS), and their size/volume fraction slightly increase with temperature and Nb content (Figure 4). Figure 5 introduces EBSD orientation maps but is not yet analyzed. Subsequent sections will likely detail texture, further TEM of κ‑carbides and NbC, and mechanical properties, as well as the selection of an appropriate aging temperature for varying aging periods. Remember: compositions, ferrite fractions, NbC presence, and the planned multi‑time aging study.
processing_route: aging at 723 K, 773 K, 823 K for 3 h
microstructure: ferrite content unchanged by aging temperature
NbC size and volume fraction slightly increase with temperature and Nb
figures_or_tables: Figure 4: NbC size and volume fraction statistics
unresolved_terms: selection of appropriate aging temperature for different aging periods
